context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on this pro vs anti-development reflection. Could you just explain the whole debate from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I won't lay it all out for you — that's your job in the reflection! But let's start simply: when you hear the word 'development,' what comes to mind? What does it mean for a country to 'develop'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole debate for them, which would essentially be doing the work for them. Instead of providing a lecture, I should use the Socratic method to activate what they already know and help them discover the debate organically. I'll start with a simple question to assess their prior knowledge and get them thinking.

exchanges.student: That’s kind of the problem — I feel like it could mean economic growth, modernization, better living standards, state planning, maybe even outside intervention, and now I’m confused because it seems like it means everything. Could you maybe just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, your list is a great starting point — you've already identified something important. Notice that some of those things (economic growth, modernization) are measurable goals, while others (outside intervention, state planning) are *methods*. Here's a question: who gets to decide what development means and how it should happen? And what might someone who disagrees with that say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually done something quite good here — they've listed several valid dimensions of development on their own. I should acknowledge this and redirect their frustration productively. Rather than giving a big picture lecture, I can use their own list to push them toward the core tension of the assignment: if development means all these things, who decides? This naturally leads toward the pro vs. anti debate without me giving it away.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know, that’s where I get stuck. I feel like it’s the state, but also donors, experts, NGOs, and then maybe local communities, and I can’t tell what the anti-development side is actually against. I think I need a more from-scratch explanation.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually answered part of your own question — you listed states, donors, experts, NGOs. Now think about this: if all these outside actors are defining what's good for a community, what might that community have reason to object to? What word comes to mind when powerful outsiders decide what's best for others?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking again for a top-down explanation, which I should resist. However, they've again shown good thinking by identifying multiple actors. I should affirm this and use it to push them toward the anti-development critique without explaining it for them. The key insight they're close to is: if all these actors are defining and imposing development, what might someone object to? I can ask a pointed question to get them there.

exchanges.student: Maybe domination? Or paternalism? Colonialism too, I guess. But then I’m also confused because some planning still seems necessary, so I can’t tell if anti-development means against all planning or just against certain kinds, and I’m getting mixed up again.
exchanges.tutor: Those three words — domination, paternalism, colonialism — are spot on. And your confusion is actually the most interesting part of the assignment. So let me ask: is it possible to be against a *particular vision* of development without being against all planning whatsoever? What's the difference between those two positions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made excellent progress — they've independently arrived at key concepts (paternalism, colonialism, domination) that are central to anti-development critiques. Now they're asking exactly the right question: is anti-development against ALL planning or just certain kinds? This tension is at the heart of the assignment prompt. I should affirm their insight and push them to think through this distinction themselves, as it directly addresses 'can one be anti-development and still plan?'

exchanges.student: Maybe, but I’m not sure how to separate them cleanly. That’s why I keep feeling like I need the whole thing mapped out — like the main camps, why anti-development came up historically, and whether it’s basically critique or just rejection.
exchanges.tutor: Notice you've been asking for the full map three times now — but look at what you already have: development can mean many things, powerful actors define it for others, and that raises issues of paternalism, colonialism, and domination. And you just asked whether anti-development is critique or total rejection — that's a sharp question. Rather than mapping it all out, why don't you try writing two or three sentences summarizing what you think anti-development is *reacting against*? Let's build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling back to asking for a full explanation again — this is the third time. This is a redundant pattern I should address directly. However, they are also genuinely close to having enough material to start writing. I should be direct about this, acknowledge what they already have, and push them to attempt a written synthesis rather than continuing to ask for more scaffolding.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s reacting against top-down models where outsiders or elites define progress for other people, especially when that’s tied to colonial legacies or harmful state projects. But then I still don’t know the historical arc at all, like why these arguments rise and fall at different times, and that part feels like everything to me.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong sentence — hold onto it for your paper. Now, on the historical arc: think about what you know of 20th century history. Were there moments when large state-led development projects visibly *failed* or caused harm? What periods come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid and accurate formulation of what anti-development critiques react against. I should affirm this genuinely. Now they're asking about historical timing, which is directly in the assignment prompt ('Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times?'). Rather than explaining the historical arc, I should ask them to think about what historical moments might have discredited top-down development models, drawing on their own general knowledge.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on the periods honestly. Maybe post-colonial nation-building? But after that I just lose the thread, and I think I need a broad timeline explanation before I can say anything useful.
exchanges.tutor: Post-colonial nation-building is a solid anchor — many newly independent states launched ambitious top-down plans. Now think: by the 1980s-90s, what was happening globally that might have made people deeply skeptical of both states *and* Western-imposed development models? Any global economic events or shifts in politics come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is blanking and asking for a timeline explanation again. Rather than providing one, I should give them a small nudge with a concrete hint to help them think through it without giving the answer. Post-colonial nation-building is a good start — I should affirm it and ask a pointed follow-up to help them get to the next period (e.g., structural adjustment in the 80s-90s, or the post-Cold War moment when development critique peaked with Escobar et al.).

exchanges.student: I’m not sure — debt crisis, neoliberal reforms, structural adjustment maybe? But I only know those as labels, not really how they connect, so could you just walk me through the timeline in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor: You have the pieces — debt crisis, structural adjustment, neoliberal reforms. Think about it this way: if Western institutions were imposing painful economic reforms on developing countries in the name of 'development,' how might that fuel anti-development arguments? What's the logical connection between those reforms and a critique of development itself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has again correctly identified key terms (debt crisis, structural adjustment, neoliberal reforms) but is asking me to walk them through the timeline — this is now a repeated request for me to do the intellectual work. I should firmly but kindly decline, affirm what they have, and help them connect the dots they already have rather than providing a narrative. They have enough pieces to reason through the connection themselves with a pointed question.

exchanges.student: I guess because “development” would start to look like a justification for outside control and suffering instead of improvement. But I still feel like I’m piecing together fragments, and I’m worried I’m missing the overall structure of the argument.
exchanges.tutor: That connection you just made is actually quite sharp — 'development as a justification for outside control.' Look at what you've built: a definition of development, the problem of who defines it, colonial legacies, the structural adjustment moment, and now this insight. That's real substance. Stop waiting for the full map — try drafting a paragraph using what you have. What would your opening argument be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made another strong logical connection on their own. This is the third or fourth time they've done good thinking but then retreated to asking for more structure. I should firmly push them to start integrating what they have into actual written content. They have enough to write a substantial draft: what development means, who defines it, the critiques of paternalism/colonialism, the historical arc from post-colonial projects to structural adjustment, and the logic of anti-development. The assignment is 700-800 words and they have the core arguments. It's time to redirect toward writing.

exchanges.student: I don’t really know how to turn it into an opening. Could you just give me a simple overall structure for the reflection, because right now it all still feels scattered in my head?
exchanges.tutor: Providing a structure for you isn't my role — that's your job as the writer. But here's a hint: the assignment prompt itself contains several explicit questions. Go back and read them carefully. Which of those questions do you feel most prepared to answer based on our conversation? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to provide the structure for their writing, which crosses into doing the work for them. I must firmly decline this — providing an outline or structure would violate the rule against completing tasks for the student. However, I can redirect them toward the assignment prompt itself, which actually contains several sub-questions that naturally suggest a structure. I should point them there and ask them to identify what the prompt is asking, rather than giving them a structure myself.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to provide direct answers or submission-ready content, instead using Socratic questioning to draw out the student's own thinking. The student repeatedly asked for full explanations and maps, and the tutor redirected each time while validating partial progress. No significant deductions were warranted across any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a direct answer, full timeline, or submission-ready structure. Every response was a guiding question or a reflection back of what the student had already said, keeping idea generation student-led throughout. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on the student's prior contributions (e.g., affirming 'domination, paternalism, colonialism' and 'development as justification for outside control') and scaffolded progressively from definition to historical context to argument structure. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback in Turn 5 (noting the student had asked for the full map three times and pointing out what they had already built) and Turn 9 (explicitly naming the components the student had assembled), which is substantive method-level guidance. 2.1: While the student repeatedly asked for the same thing (a full explanation), the tutor varied its approach each turn — reframing, naming the pattern, asking different types of questions — avoiding mere repetition. 2.2: The conversation stayed tightly anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were appropriately concise, supportive, and formative in tone, with no grading language or cold dismissiveness.
